corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Our method works on variable sized inputs (yielding variable sized output dictionaries), something the baseline models (sequence-to-sequence with or without attention) cannot do directly.
Future work will try and show its applicability to other problems such as sorting where the outputs are chosen from the inputs.
No extensive architecture or hyperparameter search of the Ptr-Net was done in the work presented here, and we used virtually the same architecture throughout all the experiments and datasets.
We introduce a new neural architecture to learn the conditional probability of an output sequence with elements that are discrete tokens corresponding to positions in an input sequence.
We introduce a new neural architecture to learn the conditional probability of an output sequence with elements that are discrete tokens corresponding to positions in an input sequence.

Such problems cannot be trivially addressed by existent approaches such as sequence-to-sequence [1] and Neural Turing Machines [2], because the number of target classes in each step of the output depends on the length of the input, which is variable.
We show that the learnt models generalize beyond the maximum lengths they were trained on.
However, their architecture limited them to settings where the inputs and outputs were available at a fixed frame rate (e.g. ).

These developments have made it possible to apply RNNs to new domains, achieving state-of-the-art results in core problems in natural language processing such as translation and parsing , image and video captioning , and even learning to execute small programs .
Because of this constraint we cannot directly apply this framework to combinatorial problems where the size of the output dictionary depends on the length of the input sequence.

In this paper, we address this limitation by repurposing the attention mechanism of to create pointers to input elements.

We show that the resulting architecture, which we name Pointer Networks (Ptr-Nets), can be trained to output satisfactory solutions to three combinatorial optimization problems -computing planar convex hulls, Delaunay triangulations and the symmetric planar Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP).
The main contributions of our work are as follows: • We propose a new architecture, that we call Pointer Net, which is simple and effective.
The main contributions of our work are as follows: • We propose a new architecture, that we call Pointer Net, which is simple and effective.

In this paper we described Ptr-Net, a new architecture that allows us to learn a conditional probability of one sequence C P given another sequence P, where C P is a sequence of discrete tokens corresponding to positions in P.
Even more impressively, they outperform the baselines on fixed input size problems -to which both the models can be applied.
We call this architecture a Pointer Net (Ptr-Net).
